scenarioName: Severe TBI with Herniation — Neuroprotective RSI

scenarioStory.history: 28-year-old male outdoor climber found unresponsive at the base of a bolted sport route by his climbing partner. Estimated 20-foot ground fall. Partner states he was belaying when the anchor failed. No helmet.
scenarioStory.discussion: Recognize signs of impending cerebral herniation (unilateral blown pupil, posturing, Cushing triad). Apply the MATC Head Injury protocol to prevent secondary brain injury. Execute neuroprotective RSI with attention to hemodynamics and ventilation targets. Understand why hyperventilation is contraindicated in TBI despite herniation signs. Coordinate airway management with spinal precautions
scenarioStory.course: Arrival — Severe TBI with Herniation Signs: Crew arrives to find patient with obvious severe head injury. Unequal pupils and decorticate posturing signal impending herniation. Sonorous airway with vomitus pooling. Spinal precautions required. Crew must recognize this as a neuroprotective RSI case and manage aggressively to prevent secondary brain injury. → Decision and Preparation for Neuroprotective RSI: Despite OPA and BVM, airway remains compromised by recurrent vomitus and the patient cannot protect his own airway. SpO2 is borderline. Crew prepares for neuroprotective RSI, verbalizing the plan, optimizing hemodynamics, and organizing the team for a controlled intubation. → Post-Intubation Neuroprotective Management: Tube confirmed. Patient paralyzed and sedated. Crew must ventilate precisely to avoid both hypoventilation (worsened ICP from hypercapnia) and hyperventilation (cerebral vasoconstriction and ischemia). Target EtCO2 40 mmHg, SBP ≥110, head of bed elevated, emergent transport to a trauma center.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Unresponsive after approximately 20-foot fall from rock climbing wall

scenarioEvents.name: Arrival — Severe TBI with Herniation Signs
scenarioEvents.description: Crew arrives to find patient with obvious severe head injury. Unequal pupils and decorticate posturing signal impending herniation. Sonorous airway with vomitus pooling. Spinal precautions required. Crew must recognize this as a neuroprotective RSI case and manage aggressively to prevent secondary brain injury.

scenarioEvents.name: Decision and Preparation for Neuroprotective RSI
scenarioEvents.description: Despite OPA and BVM, airway remains compromised by recurrent vomitus and the patient cannot protect his own airway. SpO2 is borderline. Crew prepares for neuroprotective RSI, verbalizing the plan, optimizing hemodynamics, and organizing the team for a controlled intubation.
relatedChecklist: Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography

scenarioEvents.name: Post-Intubation Neuroprotective Management
scenarioEvents.description: Tube confirmed. Patient paralyzed and sedated. Crew must ventilate precisely to avoid both hypoventilation (worsened ICP from hypercapnia) and hyperventilation (cerebral vasoconstriction and ischemia). Target EtCO2 40 mmHg, SBP ≥110, head of bed elevated, emergent transport to a trauma center.
relatedChecklist: Do NOT hyperventilate despite herniation signs

checklist.title: Maintain cervical spine immobilization throughout airway management
Jaw thrust with OPA (no NPA due to basilar skull fracture risk)
2-person BVM with PEEP at 10 breaths/min, target SpO2 ≥94% and EtCO2 35-45
Do NOT hyperventilate despite herniation signs
Perform RSI with Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg (120 mg) + Rocuronium 1 mg/kg (80 mg)
Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography
Post-intubation sedation with Midazolam 0.02 mg/kg + Fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg
Transport to Level I trauma center with early activation
Fentanyl 1 mcg/kg premedication to blunt ICP response
Apneic oxygenation via NC 15 LPM during RSI
